facts_disclosed: Studio lease renewal Sept 1, rent from $1800 to $2200

turns.content: yo I can't even focus on mixing right now I'm so stressed about this lease ting
That sounds really frustrating, especially when stress gets in the way of being creative. What's going on with the lease?
my landlord just hit me with the renewal papers for the lab and he wants to jack up the rent!! like I knew it was coming but not THIS much
Rent increases on studio spaces are really common right now unfortunately. How much of an increase are we talking about?
I'm paying $1,800 right now and he wants $2,200!! that's $400 more EVERY MONTH. like fam where am I supposed to find an extra $400 just like that
That's a significant jump -- $400 per month is $4,800 extra per year. That's real money. When does the lease come up for renewal?

September 1. so I got like 2 and a half months to figure this out. and I CANNOT lose this space still. you don't understand, I spent MONTHS doing the soundproofing in there. like actual months of work

I totally get why you don't want to lose it. Soundproofing is expensive, time-consuming, and really hard to replicate -- if you moved to a new space you'd basically be starting from scratch on all of that. A few things to consider:

1. **Negotiate** -- landlords often start high expecting pushback. Could you counter with something like $1,950 or $2,000? That shows you're flexible but $400 is too much.
2. **Longer lease term** -- offer to sign a 2-year lease in exchange for a smaller increase. Landlords like guaranteed income.
3. **Check comparable spaces** -- look at what similar commercial spaces in the area are going for. If $2,200 is above market, you have leverage.
the 2 year lease idea is SMART actually!! because he's been worried about turnover in the building, a couple other tenants left last year. so guaranteed 2 years might be worth something to him

Exactly! Tenant turnover costs landlords a lot -- they lose rent while the space sits empty, they might have to renovate, they have to find someone new. If you can frame it as "I'll give you guaranteed stability for 2 years but I need the rent to stay closer to where it is," that's a strong position.

You could propose something like: $1,900-$1,950/month on a 2-year lease. That's still an increase for him, and he gets the security of knowing the space is filled.

yeah because if I leave he's not gonna find another tenant who needs a soundproofed space like that. and honestly WHO is gonna pay $2,200 for that unit?? it's not even that big. the soundproofing is what makes it special and I DID THAT. he didn't pay for any of that!!

That's actually a really important point. If you installed the soundproofing at your own expense, you essentially improved his property for free. That's leverage too. You could mention that:

- The improvements you made increase the value of the space
- You did that work on your own dime
- If you leave, those improvements either stay (benefiting him) or you remove them (which could mean damage to the walls)

Don't phrase it as a threat, but make sure he understands the value you've added to his unit.

oooh that's a good angle still. because I literally built isolation panels and put mass loaded vinyl on the walls and EVERYTHING. that stuff is not cheap!! ok ok I'm feeling better about this. I was panicking earlier but maybe I can work this out

You definitely have more negotiating power than you think. Mass loaded vinyl, isolation panels -- that's thousands of dollars in materials alone, plus all your labor. He's benefiting from your investment.

Before you talk to him, I'd suggest:
1. Add up what you spent on the soundproofing -- have a rough number ready
2. Check listings for comparable studio spaces in your area so you know the market rate
3. Write down your counter-offer so you don't get flustered in the moment

Go in calm and prepared. You've got September 1 as your deadline so there's still time to have a proper conversation about it.

you're right. I'm gonna pull up my receipts this week and do the math on what I put into that space. then hit him with the counter offer. $1,950 on a 2 year lease plus I remind him what I invested. the RIDDIM of this negotiation is about to change ahlie!! lol thanks for talking me off the ledge

Ha, I love that energy! You went from panic to having a whole strategy in like 10 minutes. You've got a strong case -- the soundproofing investment, the long-term commitment, and the fact that he'd struggle to fill that space at $2,200. Go get your receipts together and approach it like a business conversation. You've got this. Good luck with the landlord!
